file_name: V and M - 2025 - Skin cancer detection using cnn.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and transfer learning to classify skin cancer lesions. Specifically, it employs a CNN model trained on the HAM-10000 dataset to diagnose and classify skin cancer into four categories (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Melanoma, Squamous Cell Melanoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma), achieving 96.98% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for skin cancer detection. It describes specific methods including data collection (800 images expanded to 5600 via augmentation), the use of the HAM-10000 dataset, transfer learning, and regularization techniques. The authors report new results, specifically achieving 96.98% accuracy using five-fold cross-validation, and compare their proposed model against Google Net and Mobile Net. This structure and content align with the definition of a research paper producing new results.

title: SKIN CANCER DETECTION USING CNN
journal: International Scientific Journal of Engineering and Management
brief_summary: This study proposes a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for the detection and classification of skin cancer into four categories: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Melanoma, Squamous Cell Melanoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma. The researchers utilized the publicly available HAM-10000 dataset, initially collecting 800 images which were expanded to 5600 through data augmentation techniques to prevent overfitting. The proposed deep learning architecture incorporates convolutional layers, max pooling, batch normalization, and fully connected layers, leveraging transfer learning from models pre-trained on ImageNet. Model performance was evaluated using five-fold cross-validation and compared against Google Net and Mobile Net architectures. The proposed CNN model achieved a classification accuracy of 96.98%, outperforming the two baseline models. The authors conclude that their lightweight model offers superior precision, recall, and F1 scores compared to existing state-of-the-art methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four classes used in the experiment. The paper notes it is the 'original stage of scaled cell melanoma' (likely meaning squamous cell carcinoma). Included in the HAM-10000 dataset description.

One of the four classes used in the experiment. The paper uses the term 'Scaled Cell Melanoma' in the abstract and methodology but lists 'squamous cell carcinoma' in the dataset description section. Described as creating 'scaled red marks'.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer lesion categories (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Melanoma/other classes from HAM-10000)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'diagnose Deep literacy has been extensively used in both supervised and unsupervised literacy challenges... classify Lesions in skin cancer'. The model categorizes skin lesions into specific diagnostic classes (e.g., 'rudimentary Cell Melanoma', 'Scaled Cell Melanoma', 'nasty Carcinoma', 'Actinic Keratosis') using a CNN. The performance is measured by accuracy (96.98%) and comparison of precision, recall, and F1 scores, which are standard metrics for classification tasks. The text mentions 'bracket' (likely OCR error for 'classification' or 'categorization') multiple times in the context of assigning images to groups.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large public dataset comprising dermoscopic images of various skin lesion types, obtained from Kaggle and the ISIC Archive. The paper notes it consists of 25,780 photographs of benign and malignant lesions, with metadata allowing comparisons. The authors specifically utilized a subset of 800 images which were expanded to 5600 via data augmentation for their primary experiment.

use.training: Used as the primary source for the proposed CNN model. A subset of 800 images was collected and expanded to 5600 via data augmentation for training.
use.validation: The dataset's official validation split (2167 images) was part of the overall dataset structure, but the paper states the 5600 augmented images were resolved into training and test sets. Five-fold cross-validation was used for evaluation.
use.testing: The dataset's official test split (3799 images) was part of the overall dataset structure. The paper states the 5600 augmented images were resolved into training and test sets.
use.other: Used for comparison against other algorithms (XG Boost, SVM, Random Forest) and baseline models (Google Net, Mobile Net).

HAM-10000.notes: The proposed CNN model achieved 96.98% accuracy. The exploratory analysis reveals that the proposed approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art models with substantial improvement in precision, recall, and F1 scores. The model was validated using the HAM-10000 dataset, which was expanded from 800 to 5600 images via data augmentation.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset (over a million images, 1000 classes)

training_details.transfer_learning: Yes, fine-tuned pre-trained VGG-16 on HAM-10000 by fitting fresh layers and indurating (freezing) some original layers

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed CNN model (based on VGG-16 architecture)
preprocessing_techniques: Data Augmentation (applied just-in-time during training to prevent overfitting, including horizontal flip, rotation, shear range, and zooming)
Histogram Equalization (used to improve the contrast/discrepancy of skin lesions)
Input Resizing (images resized to 224x224 pixels for the VGG-16 model input)
Dataset Splitting (HAM-10000 dataset divided into training, validation, and test sets)

conclusions: The proposed CNN model, utilizing transfer learning and data augmentation on the HAM-10000 dataset, achieved a classification accuracy of 96.98%.

The proposed lightweight CNN architecture outperformed baseline models (Google Net and Mobile Net) as well as traditional machine learning algorithms (XG Boost, SVM, Random Forest) in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores.
Traditional learning algorithms (XG Boost, SVM, Random Forest) were found to be ineffective for the classification task on the HAM-10000 dataset compared to deep learning approaches.
Data augmentation was effective in expanding the dataset from 800 to 5600 images, helping to prevent overfitting and improve model performance.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding dataset bias, class imbalance, and the interpretability of CNN models, noting that these challenges require further research for clinical implementation.
Future work should focus on improving model generalization, integrating explainable AI, and designing even lighter architectures that maintain high accuracy while minimizing computational complexity.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM-10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., melanocytic nevi account for more than half the dataset), potentially biasing the model's generalizability.
The paper acknowledges dataset limitations and class imbalance as challenges requiring further research for clinical implementation.
No external validation was performed; the model was only validated on the HAM-10000 dataset using five-fold cross-validation.
The paper notes that traditional machine learning algorithms (XG Boost, SVM, Random Forest) were ineffective for the classification task on the HAM-10000 dataset compared to deep learning approaches.
The authors identify a need for improving model generalization, integrating explainable AI, and addressing dataset limitations for real-world deployment.
The study does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for the comparison between the proposed model and baselines.

The paper mentions that the proposed lightweight CNN architecture offers superior precision, recall, and F1 scores compared to existing state-of-the-art methods, but specific details on computational complexity metrics (beyond 'lightweight') are not provided.
